summary: Ell Persons, a Black woodcutter, was lynched on May 22, 1917, near Memphis, Tennessee, after being accused of murdering a white teenager. A mob seized him while in custody, burned him alive before a large crowd, and displayed his remains publicly. No one was ever charged, and the atrocity helped catalyze the founding of the Memphis NAACP chapter.

historicalContext: The lynching of Ell Persons occurred during the Nadir of American race relations, a period marked by widespread racial terror and the institutionalization of Jim Crow. Such lynchings were acts of racial control, often met with official complicity or indifference, and served to reinforce segregation and disenfranchisement. In this case, the public spectacle and lack of accountability underscore the failure of legal protections for Black citizens.

summary: Benny Richards, a Black farmer, was lynched on May 2, 1919, in Warrenton, Georgia, by a mob of 300 white men after being accused of killing his ex-wife. His body was shot and burned, an act of racial terror documented in historical accounts of Jim Crow–era violence in Warren County.

historicalContext: Lynching was a tool of racial control during the Jim Crow era, used to enforce white supremacy and suppress Black autonomy. The extrajudicial killing of Benny Richards in 1919 reflects the widespread pattern of mob violence in rural Georgia, where legal institutions routinely failed to protect Black citizens or prosecute perpetrators.

summary: Elias Clayton, an African American circus worker, was lynched by a white mob in Duluth, Minnesota, on June 15, 1920, following false accusations of rape. Alongside [[lynching_elmer_jackson_duluth_minnesota|Elmer Jackson]] and [[lynching_isaac_mcghie_duluth_minnesota|Isaac McGhie]], he was taken from jail, beaten, and hanged.

historicalContext: The 1920 Duluth lynchings occurred during the Red Summer and the Great Migration, when racial tensions flared as Black workers moved North and competed with white ethnics for jobs. This lynching, rare in the Midwest, exemplifies how mob violence enforced racial hierarchy beyond the South, contributing to national debates over civil rights and state responsibility.

summary: Elmer Jackson, an African American circus worker, was lynched on June 15, 1920, in Duluth, Minnesota, by a white mob falsely accusing him and two others of assaulting a white woman. Taken from jail, he was beaten and hanged without trial. The incident spurred Minnesota’s 1921 anti-lynching law and was memorialized in 2003.
locationLabel: Site of the 1920 Duluth lynching near the downtown jail

historicalContext: The lynching of Elmer Jackson occurred during the Nadir of American race relations, a period marked by widespread anti-Black violence and racial scapegoating, particularly as Black migrants moved North during the Great Migration. In industrial cities like Duluth, competition for jobs heightened racial tensions, enabling mob violence despite the absence of evidence. The event catalyzed state-level legal reform, reflecting how such atrocities sometimes prompted institutional responses, albeit without accountability for the perpetrators.

summary: Isaac McGhie, a Black circus worker, was lynched by a white mob in Duluth, Minnesota, on June 15, 1920, after false accusations of rape. Alongside [[lynching_elias_clayton_duluth_minnesota|Elias Clayton]] and [[lynching_elmer_jackson_duluth_minnesota|Elmer Jackson]], he was taken from jail, beaten, and hanged. No one was convicted of murder, though three were jailed for rioting.

historicalContext: The 1920 Duluth lynchings occurred during the Nadir of American race relations, a period marked by widespread anti-Black violence and the erosion of civil rights. This event reflects the racial tensions exacerbated by the Great Migration and white working-class fears of economic competition, set against a backdrop of systemic failures to protect Black lives. The lack of murder convictions and the subsequent passage of anti-lynching legislation illustrate both the entrenched nature of racial terror and the limited legal responses to it.

summary: Lige Daniels was lynched on August 3, 1920, in Center, Texas (Shelby County), after being accused of murdering a white woman. A mob of approximately 1,000 people seized him from jail and hanged him in the town square, an act of racial terror consistent with Jim Crow-era violence targeting Black Americans.

claims.object: on August 3, 1920, in Center, Texas, following an accusation of murdering a white woman
by a mob of approximately 1,000 people and hanged in the town square of Center, Texas

historicalContext: Lynching was a tool of racial control in the Jim Crow South, used to enforce white supremacy and suppress Black autonomy. The extrajudicial killing of Black people—often under accusations of crimes against white individuals—was frequently carried out with community complicity and impunity. Center, Texas, located in a region with a history of racial violence, exemplifies how local institutions failed to protect Black residents from mob rule.

summary: Julius "July" Perry, a prosperous Black farmer in Ocoee, Florida, was lynched on November 3, 1920, after attempting to vote. His home was attacked by a white mob during the Ocoee massacre, a violent suppression of Black suffrage that killed dozens and expelled nearly all African Americans from the town. A sign on his body read, "This is what we do to niggers that vote."

historicalContext: The lynching of July Perry occurred during the Ocoee massacre, a direct assault on Black political participation in the Jim Crow South. Amid widespread voter suppression, Black citizens like Perry who challenged disenfranchisement through voter registration drives became targets of racial terror. The massacre exemplifies how economic jealousy, white supremacist violence, and electoral politics converged to maintain racial hierarchy.
